did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes a protocol for a clinical trial evaluating an artificial intelligence diagnostic aid for skin cancer. The AI method is a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) developed by MoleMap Ltd and Monash eResearch, which classifies dermoscopic skin lesions as benign, malignant, or uncertain. The study aims to obtain results regarding the AI's performance (sensitivity, specificity, agreement with histopathology) and its impact on dermatologists' diagnostic and management decisions in a specialist dermatology setting.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Protocol' for a 'preintervention/postintervention trial' (SMARTI). It describes original experimental methods, including participant recruitment, AI algorithm implementation (CNN), data collection, and statistical analysis plans for a clinical trial. Although it does not report final results, it outlines a study designed to produce new empirical data, fitting the definition of a research paper (specifically a study protocol) rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Improving Skin cancer Management with ARTificial Intelligence (SMARTI): protocol for a preintervention/ postintervention trial of an artificial intelligence system used as a diagnostic aid for skin cancer management in a specialist dermatology setting
brief_summary: This paper presents the protocol for the SMARTI study, a preintervention/postintervention clinical trial evaluating an artificial intelligence diagnostic aid for skin cancer management in a specialist dermatology setting. The AI system, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) developed by MoleMap Ltd and Monash eResearch, classifies dermoscopic lesions as benign, malignant, or uncertain. Participants are recruited consecutively from melanoma and skin cancer assessment clinics at the Alfred Hospital and Skin Health Institute in Melbourne. The study compares the AI's performance against teledermatologists, registrars, and histopathology results, while also assessing the AI's impact on clinicians' diagnostic and management decisions. The primary analysis measures the AI's sensitivity, specificity, and agreement with histopathology or consultant dermatologists' classifications. The trial aims to validate the safety and utility of the AI aid in a real-world clinical environment prior to potential implementation in primary care.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the 'Malignant' classification category. The study population includes patients from melanoma and skin cancer assessment clinics. Histopathology results for biopsied lesions serve as the reference standard.
Included in the 'Malignant' classification category (Table 1). Evaluated via histopathology for biopsied lesions.

paper_terms: any skin lesion about which the clinician is not confident with regards to its benign/malignant status
lesion_types_studied.details: A specific diagnostic class used by the AI and clinicians to categorize lesions that are not definitely benign or malignant (e.g., severely dysplastic naevi or low grade actinic keratoses).

all_task_types.target: Lesion diagnostic category (benign, malignant, or uncertain)
all_task_types.details: The AI algorithm, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), classifies dermoscopic skin lesions into three discrete diagnostic categories: benign, malignant, or uncertain. The study evaluates the accuracy of this classification against histopathology and dermatologist assessments using sensitivity, specificity, and kappa statistics.

datasets_used.name: SMARTI Study Cohort (Alfred Hospital and Skin Health Institute)
datasets_used.description: A prospective cohort of patients recruited consecutively from melanoma and skin cancer assessment clinics at the Alfred Hospital and Skin Health Institute in Melbourne. Participants are adults aged 18+ with at least one skin lesion of concern and willing to have multiple lesions imaged. Acral and scalp lesions are excluded. This dataset is used to evaluate the real-world performance of the AI diagnostic aid.

use.testing: The dataset serves as the primary evaluation cohort for the clinical trial. AI performance (sensitivity, specificity, agreement with histopathology/consultant dermatologists) is measured on these lesions. Images collected during the preintervention period are explicitly not used for algorithm retraining.
use.other: The study compares AI classifications against teledermatologist assessments, registrar assessments, and histopathology results for biopsied lesions.

datasets_used.name: MoleMap Ltd Proprietary Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of macroscopic and polarised dermoscopic images from which the CNN algorithm was originally trained and tested in a laboratory setting. The paper notes that the algorithm was tested with different images to those with which it was trained, but both were derived from this same dataset.

use.training: Used to train the initial version of the CNN algorithm (which demonstrated sensitivity of 85%, specificity of 78%, AUROC 0.91 for melanoma detection in a lab setting).
use.testing: Used for initial laboratory testing of the first version of the CNN against teledermatologist classifications.
use.other: The algorithm used in the postintervention period of the SMARTI study is an updated version of the CNN, selected based on interim quality assurance analysis for greatest accuracy, but the specific split of this proprietary dataset is not detailed in the protocol.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate agreement between benign/uncertain/malignant lesion classifications by the AI algorithm and physician assessors or histopathology results.
Used for kappa calculation to account for the degree of disagreement in the ordinal classification of lesions.
To evaluate the discriminatory ability of the AI algorithm for malignant lesions, including sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive values, and negative predictive values.
Used in sample size calculations to estimate AI algorithm lesion classification accuracy with reasonable precision (targeting a 95% CI of ±0.05 for a kappa statistic of 0.8).

To supplement the primary analysis by assuming opposite status for lesions where follow-up is unavailable (e.g., considering a lesion planned for biopsy as malignant if histopathology is not available, and vice versa).

To evaluate the safety of the AI algorithm by assessing its agreement with consultant dermatologists' classifications and histopathology results for biopsied/excised lesions before implementing the algorithm in the postintervention period.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper is a study protocol and does not report results of formal hypothesis testing. It outlines plans to use kappa statistics, sensitivity, specificity, and confidence intervals to evaluate AI performance, but no p-values or significance tests are reported.

MoleMap Ltd Proprietary Dataset (Laborat.notes: Results from a laboratory setting for distinguishing a 'cancer' from a benign lesion in a binary decision task.

SMARTI Study Cohort (Alfred Hospital and.notes: The paper is a protocol for a preintervention/postintervention trial. It outlines the plan to evaluate the AI's sensitivity, specificity, and agreement with histopathology/consultant dermatologists, but does not report the final quantitative results of this clinical trial.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a proprietary dataset of macroscopic and polarised dermoscopic images from MoleMap Ltd. Both macroscopic and dermoscopic images were used for training. The algorithm was tested with different images to those with which it was trained, but derived from the same dataset.

architecture_details.classification_scale: Three-point scale: benign, malignant, or uncertain
architecture_details.notes: The specific CNN architecture (e.g., ResNet, VGG, etc.), layer count, or parameter counts are not reported in the protocol. An updated version of the CNN was selected for the postintervention period based on interim quality assurance analysis for greatest accuracy.

training_details.testing_method: Tested with different images to those with which it was trained

preprocessing_techniques: Image acquisition using a proprietary dermoscopic camera capable of taking macroscopic and polarised dermoscopic images
Uploading of images to an electronic Case Report Form (eCRF)
Recording of lesion location on a digital avatar
conclusions: The study is a protocol for a preintervention/postintervention clinical trial and does not report final results or definitive conclusions regarding the AI's performance or clinical impact.

The authors acknowledge that while Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) can diagnose skin cancers with impressive accuracy in experimental settings, their performance in real-world clinical settings has not been validated in prospective clinical studies.
The study aims to evaluate the safety and performance of an AI diagnostic aid for skin cancer detection and management in a real-world specialist dermatology setting.

The authors acknowledge that inherent differences in the application of AI in a specialist setting may limit the generalizability of the study findings to primary care settings, necessitating further research to establish feasibility for broader clinical implementation.
important_remarks: The paper is a study protocol and does not report final results, sensitivity, specificity, or clinical impact metrics for the SMARTI trial.
The AI algorithm was trained on a proprietary dataset from MoleMap Ltd; the specific architecture, hyperparameters, and training split details are not reported.

An updated version of the CNN was selected for the post-intervention period based on interim quality assurance analysis, but the specific changes or performance of this updated model are not detailed.

The study population is recruited from specialist dermatology and melanoma clinics in Melbourne, which may limit generalizability to primary care settings due to differences in disease prevalence and clinician experience.
Lesions labeled 'benign' that are not actively monitored have unknown ultimate malignancy status, creating a potential limitation in evaluating false negatives for benign-classified lesions.
Several authors have competing interests, including personal fees and shareholdings in MoleMap Ltd, the developer of the AI system.

The study design compares AI performance against teledermatologists, registrars, and histopathology, but does not account for additional clinical information (e.g., patient demographics) available to face-to-face dermatologists in real-world practice.
Acral and scalp lesions are excluded from the study, limiting the scope of the evaluation to other skin sites.
